coredata.dc:title: Optical behaviour of nematic liquid crystal doped with carbon dot in the nonlinear optical regime
coredata.dc:creator: Praseetha, K.P
Chandrasekharan, K.
Varghese, Soney
coredata.dc:description: We delve into the effect of carbon dot (c-dot) doping at different concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 1 wt%) on the third order nonlinear optical properties of a nematic liquid crystal (NLC). C-dots were synthesized by the thermal decomposition of ammonium citrate salt. The size and morphology of prepared c-dots were confirmed by the TEM micrograph. Nonlinear optical properties of c-dot doped liquid crystal were investigated by the z-scan method using a Q-switched Nd: YAG laser delivering 7 ns pulse at 532 nm wavelength. A phenomenal increase in the nonlinear absorption coefficient, βeff, was observed for 0.2 wt% c-dot doped liquid crystal. There is an enhancement in the value of third order susceptibility, χ(3) , of the order of 10 for the doped liquid crystal. An increase in the third order nonlinear optical absorption coefficient implies a better optical limiting characteristic than the pristine LC. The optical limiting threshold value of 0.2 wt% c-dot doped LC is found to be 1.23 J/cm2. We determine for the first time that c-dot doping in NLC leads to a significant improvement of the nonlinear optical properties compared to pure LC. The results indicate that the system under study could be a promising candidate for nonlinear photonic devices and optical limiters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nonlinear optics
Carbon dot
Liquid crystal
Optical limiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible and fluorescence spectra of C dot and the inset shows a photographic image of c-dot under ultraviolet irradiation.
(a) TEM image of c-dot and HRTEM image(inset) shows the interplanar distance of synthesized c-dot (b) XRD of c-dot.
Open aperture curve of pure 5PCH LC and c-dot doped LC.
Closed aperture curve of various wt% c-dot doped 5PCH.
Optical limiting curve of pure and doped 5PCH LC.
Obtained values βeff , and calculated values of χ(3) and nonlinear refractive index of the samples understudy.

head.ce:title: Optical behaviour of nematic liquid crystal doped with carbon dot in the nonlinear optical regime

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterial and Devices Research Laboratory, School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology Calicut, Kerala 673601, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanomaterial and Devices Research Laboratory, School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Kerala 673601, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser and Nonlinear Optics Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut, Kerala 673601, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laser and Nonlinear Optics Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut, Kerala 673601, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanomaterial and Devices Research Laboratory, School of Materials science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology Calicut, Kerala 673601, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We delve into the effect of carbon dot (c-dot) doping at different concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 1 wt%) on the third order nonlinear optical properties of a nematic liquid crystal (NLC). C-dots were synthesized by the thermal decomposition of ammonium citrate salt. The size and morphology of prepared c-dots were confirmed by the TEM micrograph. Nonlinear optical properties of c-dot doped liquid crystal were investigated by the z-scan method using a Q-switched Nd: YAG laser delivering 7 ns pulse at 532 nm wavelength. A phenomenal increase in the nonlinear absorption coefficient, βeff, was observed for 0.2 wt% c-dot doped liquid crystal. There is an enhancement in the value of third order susceptibility, χ(3) , of the order of 10 for the doped liquid crystal. An increase in the third order nonlinear optical absorption coefficient implies a better optical limiting characteristic than the pristine LC. The optical limiting threshold value of 0.2 wt% c-dot doped LC is found to be 1.23 J/cm2. We determine for the first time that c-dot doping in NLC leads to a significant improvement of the nonlinear optical properties compared to pure LC. The results indicate that the system under study could be a promising candidate for nonlinear photonic devices and optical limiters.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid crystals (LC) constitute an enchanting class of material that behaves like fluids and simultaneously possess characteristics of solids. Beyond display technology, LC finds plenty of applications in the different areas of fundamental science due to its unique and attractive optical and electrical properties. NLCs are anisotropic, and their molecules are oriented along a particular direction called the director (n). Since LCs are highly anisotropic and possess high correlated molecular reorientation, they exhibit strong nonlinear optical properties. Hence besides displays, the NLC provides a space for nonlinear optics [1]. Photorefractive properties, orientational effects produced by laser irradiation in dye-doped liquid crystals etc. are vitally exciting and practically useful phenomena.

The modification of optical and electrical properties of NLC by the addition of organic dyes and nanomaterial has become the subject of intense study in recent years. Manipulating the properties of LC material through this method provides a significant improvement in the performance of the LC-based device and display application. Nowadays, research on doping nanoparticle (NP) gets relevance, since it is possible to get new nanomaterials, that are varying in shape, size and composition. Doping with a minimal concentration of NP is an adequate way to enhance the optical and electro-optical (EO) properties of the NLC [2,3]. Doping LC with metallic NP such as Ag and Au can modify EO properties such as response time and threshold voltage, thereby improving display characteristics of the LC [4–7]. Studies on NP-LC composites are not only limited, doping with metallic NP but also with semiconductor quantum dot, ferroelectric and magnetic NPs that alters nonlinear optical properties such as two beam coupling, nonlinear absorption (NLA), nonlinear refraction (NLR) and photorefractive efficiency [8–10]. Though research on nonlinear optical properties of the nanomaterial doped LC exhibited interesting results, carbonaceous material doped LC nanocomposite is rarely studied in the nonlinear regime. In this article, we discuss findings on nonlinear optical properties of the nanocolloid prepared by the dispersion of c-dot in NLC, 4-(trans-4-Pentylcyclohexyl) benzonitrile, (5 PCH).

C-dots are a class of carbon nanomaterial, usually of size less than 10 nm. They attained ample attention in the field of research, owing to their excellent photostability and favourable biocompatibility. C-dots possess fascinating optical and EO properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects [11,12]. This nanomaterial owns unique advantages over semiconductor quantum dots because of its benign, abundant, and inexpensive nature. C-dots act as reasonable candidates for future nanodevices since it has robust optical and chemical inertness and can be prepared by straight forward synthesis [13,14]. Photoluminescent carbon-based quantum dots have high resistance to photobleaching compared to organic dyes [15]. The mechanism behind the photoluminescent properties of the c-dot was a mystery when it was observed for the first time. C-dots possess excellent electronic properties that made them an inevitable part of different types of sensors [16,17]. Also, their electroluminescence characteristics can be used in optronics [18,19].

From the literature, it can be seen that c-dot has wide application in the field of solar cells, supercapacitors, and used as light emitting diodes [19,20]. Taking into account all these dazzling optical properties of c-dots and in the light of significant results of earlier reports of carbon NPs/LC nanocolloids [21–23], the present study is concerned with the nonlinear optical effects of organophilic c-dot doping on the NLC's properties.

C-dots are synthesized in different ways such as laser ablation, thermal oxidation of carbonaceous compounds, electrochemical method. But the outcome of most of these routes is hydrophilic c-dot. Since, here the host material is a liquid crystal, c-dots, which are organophilic in nature will be a better choice. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the synthesis of organophilic c-dots has been accomplished by slightly modifying previously reported methods [24]. The precursor used for the preparation of c-dots are citric acid monohydrate (Merk), and 1-octadecylamine (Alfa Aesar) and ethanol as solvent. The mixture of citric acid and ethanol was stirred for one hour, followed by the addition octadecylamine dispersed in ethanol to the mixture. The obtained suspension was centrifuged to collect the deposit. The precipitate was washed with ethanol and dried. The salt was then directly calcined in air at 275 °C for two hours at a heating rate of 10 °C/minute. The resulting solid was dispersed in a small amount of methanol and repeatedly washed with acetone and ethanol.

The UV–Visible spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of synthesized c-dots were done on Shimadzu UV1800 UV spectrometer and Perkin Elmer LS45, respectively. TEM analysis was done on a JEOL JEM 2100 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained from Rigaku Minflex 600 using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried on a Perkin Elmer Frontier spectrometer with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1.

Nonlinear optical features of the samples were measured by z-scan technique, using Q-switched Nd: YAG laser (Quanta-Ray INDI), delivering pulses of width 7 ns (FWHM) and second harmonic wavelength of 532 nm at a repetition rate of 10 Hz. This technique helps to determine the sign and magnitude of NLA and NLR simultaneously. The z-scan experiment for open aperture (OA) and closed aperture (CA) measurement were done according to the procedure outlined in [25] with the incident intensity of 15 µJ. Energy ratio meter Rj-7620 (Laser Probe Inc, USA) was used to compare input and output pulses. To minimise the cumulative thermal effect, we conducted the z-scan experiments at a lower intensity. Also, at each step of the translational motion, laser pulses are incident on the sample in a single shot mode so that the thermal expansion is negligible. During the experiment, we couldn’t find any formation of diffraction ring as a consequence of stronger thermal expansion, which indicates a negligible cumulative thermal effect [26,27]. A schematic diagram of the z-scan experimental set-up is given in Fig. 2 . The room temperature LC 5PCH (clearing temperature TC = 57 °C), used for the experiment was supplied by TCI Chemicals, India. Fig. 3 illustrates the molecular structure of the LC under study.

The current investigation is focussed on the third order NLO properties of c-dot dispersed in the 5PCH LC. Dispersion of c-dot in four different weight percentages (0.1, 0.2, 0.4 and 1 wt%) in NLC is prepared. For this, c-dot was first dispersed in toluene, and then a definite volume concentration is mixed with pure LC. The doped samples were kept under ultrasonication for 2 h, followed by intermittent probe sonication for 30 s with10 s interval, and kept in the oven until the solvent is evaporated. The samples are probe sonicated again and LC cells are filled with these samples at a temperature higher than the isotropic temperature.

The process of making planar aligned LC cell is reported in our previous article [28]. Briefly, LC cells are made up of two ITO glass plates whose conducting surfaces were spin-coated with polyimide to promote the planar alignment of the LC molecule. The spin-coated surface was cured and buffed uniaxially with a velvet cloth. The cleaned, spin-coated ITO plates are glued together in antiparallel rubbing direction with a cell gap made by a spacer of 25 µm thickness.

Fig. 4 illustrates the linear absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of prepared c-dots. The absorption maximum is obtained at a wavelength of 277 nm, which is associated with the n-π* transition of CO [29]. The emission peak was obtained at 422 nm at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Inset shows a photographic image of the c-dots under ultraviolet irradiation.

The surface component of the synthesized c-dot is examined by FTIR spectroscopy. As revealed by Fig. 5 , a broad band observed near 3421 cm−1 originated from NH stretching. The absorption bands at 2973 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching vibration of the aliphatic group. The peak at 1650 cm−1 can be attributed to amide linkage. The absorption band at 1249 cm−1 shows the presence of CN stretching in amine and the peak at 1051 cm−1 indicates CO stretching vibration [30].

The TEM image depicted in Fig. 6 (a) confirmed the presence of very small sized spherical c-dot. The size of the c-dots ranges from 8 to 10 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) taken from one NP (inset of Fig. 6) exhibits a crystalline structure with lattice spacing 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (1 0 0) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [11,31].

XRD pattern, as illustrated in Fig. 6(b) displays a broad peak centred at 2θ = 22.2°, suggesting a lattice spacing distance of 4A°, which explains the graphite like structure of prepared c-dot. The reason for peak broadening in the XRD pattern is attributed to the small crystallite size of the c-dots [31,32].

The determination of third order susceptibility involved two-stage measurements of real and imaginary parts. In the OA study, the variation of transmitted intensity through the sample is recorded. The sample is placed on a translational stage and a computer controls its movement. The specimen moves in the direction of incident focussed light such that the intensity of transmitted light is varied at each position(z). Then the nonlinear optical absorption coefficient βeff can be determined by fitting the data accessed from OA experiment using the following relation [25],
(1)
T

=

π
1 / 2

∫
+ ∞
- ∞
l n
1 + q
( z , 0 )

- τ
2
d τ
where q(z) is given by the relation
(2)
q
z , 0
=
β
eff
I 0
1 -
e
-
α
0 L
1 +
z

2

where βeff is the effective two-photon absorption coefficient, L sample thickness, I0 is the on-axis input intensity, and α0 is the linear absorption coefficient. The Rayleigh range is given by
z 0
= π
ω
0
2
/ λ
with ω0 being the beam waist radius and λ is the wavelength of the laser.

The NLA properties of the c-dot–LC samples were studied at an incident intensity of 15 µJ. From Fig. 7 , it is evident that as the LC cell advances towards the focal plane, the normalized transmittance reduces, which shows the intensity-dependent NLA property of the sample. In Fig. 7, the data points for a particular system are given by a specific symbol, and the solid curve represents theoretical fitting by a nonlinear wave equation employing two photon absorption (TPA).

It is clear from the OA curve that pure LC shows a transmittance minimum at the focal point, characteristics of reverse saturable absorption (RSA). The NLA increases with an increment in doping percentage. The absorption is maximum for 0.2 wt% c-dot doped samples. Further increase in the doping percentage reduces the NLA property.

Extracting NLA coefficient facilitates the estimation of the imaginary part of third order susceptibility (Im χ(3)) from the relation [33]
(3)
Im
χ
( 3 )
=
n
0
2
ε 0
c λ
β
eff
/ 2 π
The values of βeff obtained for pure and c-dot doped LC from the OA curve fitting and corresponding calculated values of Im χ (3) are given in Table 1 .

The origin of the induced NLA property of the c-dot-LC system can be explained as follows. The features of c-dots can be considered analogous to those of inorganic quantum dot [34,35]. Hence, as laser radiation is incident on the c-dots, electrons from the valence band get excited, leading to the generation of a large number of free carriers, they, in turn, get excited further to higher levels by absorbing photons [36]. This can be a reason for the NLO properties of the c-dot. Also, in the case of NLC, the collective optical nonlinearities are associated with the director axis reorientation, which is mainly due to the birefringence of NLC [37,38]. From the linear absorption spectrum of the c-dot, it is clear that the absorption peak is at 277 nm, which is almost half of the excitation wavelength of the laser source. Further, the absorption maximum of pure LC is also falling in the same region. This result indicates a chance of TPA to occur, one of the vital processes of NLA. Generally, TPA is observed at higher incident energy attained by picosecond and femtosecond laser pulses. However, significant NLA observed in the pure NLC suggested that excited state absorption (ESA) may be the responsible mechanism [39]. But when we consider a guest-host (c-dot-NLC) system, the enhancement in the NLA can be due to anomalous optical reorientation in doped NLC, which happens as a result of the internal interaction of the excited c-dot and the nematic host [40].

As depicted in Fig. 7, NLA increases until the doping percentage increases to 0.2 wt%. But for 0.4 wt% and higher weight percentages of c dot, βeff decreases. This reduction can be attributed to the agglomeration of c-dot at higher weight percentages and it affects the orientation of the LC molecule in the presence of an optical field [41].

The normalized transmittance data obtained from the CA procedure is fitted to the expression [42]
(4)
T
z
= 1 +
4 x
( x
2
+ 9 )

x
2
+ 1 )
Δ
∅ 0
where
x = z /
z 0
, and Δϕ0 is the on-axis nonlinear phase shift. The NLR coefficient can be calculated applying the equation,
(5)
n 2
e s u
=
c
n 0
λ Δ
ϕ 0
80
π
2
I 0
L
eff

From which real part of third order nonlinear susceptibility is obtained using the formula
Re
χ
3
( e s u )
= 2
n
0
2
ε 0
c
n 2
. Fig. 8 illustrates the CA z-scan plot of the c-dot doped 5PCH LC samples, which exhibits a prefocal transmission peak and post focal transmission valley, indicatives of negative refractive nonlinearity of the compound which is ascribed to the self-defocusing effect of the Gaussian beam within the sample. It is clear from the CA plot that the self-defocusing effect is enhanced until the c-dot doping percentage reaches to 0.2 wt%. Then the effect is found to be reduced beyond the concentration of 0.2 wt% of c-dot. From Table 1, we can see that third order susceptibility for the 0.2 wt% c-dot doped LC is 1.519 × 10−17 m2/V2 with the real and the imaginary part of χ (3) are equal to −1.3 × 10−19 m2/V2 and 1.518 × 10−17 m2/V2 respectively. Our results are comparable or better than already reported values [9,43]. Specifically, in the study of NLO features of π- conjugated benzodifuran-based derivatives, authors obtained the highest value of χ (3) for S5 derivative as 25 × 10−21 m2/V2 [44]. NLO properties of a material can be significantly enhanced by annealing process and also metal induced enhancement of NLO in azo-based iminopyridine complexes are also found in the literature [45–49]. We can claim that the obtained values of NLO parameters from our investigations are higher or comparable to those of the reported values. The increase in the NLR and hence, a high value of n2 is due to the internal interaction between LC and c-dot. A few works of literature mentioned on the charge transfer mechanism between the carbonaceous nanomaterial, such as carbon nanotube, fullerene, graphene, and the LC, which substantially reduces the free ion concentration in the LC through ion trapping [21,50]. Hence, the enhancement in the NLR is attributed to both the charge transfer mechanism and the Janossy effect [51]. The phenomenon of the self-aggregation of c-dot at higher concentrations, which causes an interruption in the optical reorientation of NLC, accounts for the reduction of NLO properties beyond 0.2 wt% doping of c-dot in LC [52–54].

An ideal optical limiter is one that exhibits stable normalized transmittance at small input fluence and a diminished transmittance at large input fluence [55–57]. The OL phenomenon will start acting as soon as the input fluence exceeds the threshold energy, prescribed for the material. The incident beam initiates absorptive and refractive properties within the material resulting in a significant reduction in the energy of the emergent beam. Different phenomena like ESA, TPA, RSA and free-carrier absorption are responsible for the OL action of the material [58].

Fig. 9 illustrates the OL behaviour of 5PCH LC doped with different concentrations of c-dot. Usually, the performance of an optical limiter is rated by its OL threshold value. It is defined as the value of input fluence at which the normalized transmittance reduced to 50%. A better optical limiter will be having a lower threshold value for the OL behaviour of the samples was derived from the OA z-scan data and is depicted in Fig. 9. It can be seen that the OL property of pure LC does not reach the threshold limit.

Power limiting performance was improving with increasing the concentration of c-dot, which was in agreement with the results of OA z-scan measurement. OL curve shows that 0.2 and 0.4 wt% c-dot doped samples have an OL threshold of 1.23 J/cm2 and 2.99 J/cm2, respectively. This is a better value for an optical limiter. This result proves that 0.2 wt% doped LC is the best optical limiter out of the samples under consideration, as demonstrated by the results of OA.

The OL property of the sample can be attributed to the interplay between the Janossy effect and the charge transfer between LC and c-dot. However, when doping percentage increases above 0.2%, the NLA and hence OL property seems to be reducing. Agglomeration of the c-dots become significant at this doping percentage, which disturbs the reorientation of the LC molecule, followed by a reduction in the OL property [59–61]. The OL threshold values of the c-dot-LC system are tantamount to that of many other materials which are suggested as prime optical limiting materials [57]. Hence the studied system can be considered as an admissible nominee for OL applications.

Organophilic c-dots are synthesized and characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The spherical morphology of the c-dot is confirmed by TEM. The size of the c-dot was within the range of 8–10 nm. Third order optical analysis of the c-dot-LC system in the nonlinear optical regime is studied. There is a remarkable enhancement in the NLA coefficient on doping the c-dot in NLC. The RSA observed in the c-dot-LC system is associated with the combined effect of the ESA, taking place in NLC along with the TPA in c -dot. Also, in the guest-host system (c-dot-5PCH LC), we expect an anomalous reorientation of the LC molecule due to the interaction of LC molecule and excited state c-dot, which highly contributes to the observed enhancement of NLO properties. The self-defocussing property of the pure and doped NLC is analysed by CA z-scan investigation. The notable enhancement in the NLR of the c-dot doped LC can be explained by two effects, the Janossy effect and charge-transfer mechanism occurring between the c-dot and LC molecule. Janossy effect arises due to the difference in molecular interaction between guest-host system when the dopant is in the ground and the excited state. An optical threshold of 1.23 J/cm2 was detected from the OL behaviour of the system. This threshold value suggests that the system under study can find application in optical sensor protection and photonic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge NIT, Calicut, Kozhikode, Kerala, India, for financial support. Praseetha K. P. expresses her gratitude towards Mr. E. Shiju for his valid suggestions and support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optlastec.2020.106367.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
